items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.
The answer examiners want is: Augmentation. “Diminution” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Reversing the order of pitches is:
items.why: Correct answer: Retrograde. “Inversion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a motif is sequenced at every step of a 4-note ascending pattern, how many statements result?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
